title: String Functions
description:
We consider a string of n characters, indexed 1,2,\dots,n. Your task is to calculate all values of the following functions:

- z(i) denotes the maximum length of a substring that begins at position i and is a prefix of the string. In addition, z(1)=0.
- \pi(i) denotes the maximum length of a substring that ends at position i, is a prefix of the string, and whose length is at most i-1.

The input has one line containing a string of lowercase letters a–z.

Output two lines: first the values of z for i=1..n, then the values of \pi for i=1..n.

examples.explanation: First line lists z(1..7), second line lists \pi(1..7).

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "ab", what are z(1) and z(2)?
mcqChain.explanation: By definition z(1)=0. Starting at i=2, substring is "b" and the prefix starts with "a", so z(2)=0.

wrongExplanations.1: z(1) is defined as 0 regardless of the string.
wrongExplanations.2: Starting at position 2, 'b' does not match the first character 'a'.

mcqChain.hint: z(i) compares s[i..] with the prefix s[1..] (1-based).

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "aaaaa", what is z(2)? (Indices are 1-based.)
mcqChain.explanation: Starting at i=2, the substring is "aaaa" which matches the prefix "aaaa" of the whole string; length is 4.

wrongExplanations.2: You can match all 4 remaining characters, not just 3.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "abab", what is z(3)? (1-based indexing.)
mcqChain.explanation: Starting at i=3, substring is "ab" and prefix is "ab..." so the maximum match length is 2.

wrongExplanations.1: Both characters match, so 2 is achievable.
wrongExplanations.2: At least 1 character ('a') matches at position 3.

mcqChain.hint: Compare s[3..] with s[1..].

mcqChain.questionText: Is z(1) ever nonzero?

options.label: Yes, equals n if all characters are the same.
Yes, equals n-1 if first and last match.
Only if the string is a palindrome.

mcqChain.explanation: The problem defines z(1)=0 always.

wrongExplanations.1: Even if the string matches itself, z(1) is defined to be 0.
wrongExplanations.2: Definition does not depend on palindrome or edges.

mcqChain.hint: Read the definition carefully.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "ababa", what is π(5)? (π(i) is the length of the longest prefix that ends at i and is at most i−1.)
mcqChain.explanation: s[1..3] = "aba" also ends at position 5 as a suffix, so length 3.

wrongExplanations.1: It cannot be 4 because the prefix length must be at most i−1.
wrongExplanations.2: There is a longer match than 2: "aba".

mcqChain.hint: Check all proper prefixes that are also suffixes of s[1..5].

mcqChain.questionText: If you compute each z(i) by comparing s[i..] to the prefix from scratch, what is the worst-case time over the whole string?
mcqChain.explanation: In strings like "aaaa...", each position can scan many characters, summing to ~n^2.

wrongExplanations.2: You would need to reuse previous work to get linear time.

mcqChain.hint: Consider "aaaa..." and how many comparisons happen.

mcqChain.questionText: If you compute each π(i) by checking all prefix lengths ending at i, what is the worst-case time?
mcqChain.explanation: Trying all shorter lengths at each i can lead to quadratic behavior.

wrongExplanations.1: Linear time requires carrying forward partial results instead of restarting.

mcqChain.hint: Worst-case strings cause many fallbacks if you do it naively.

mcqChain.questionText: We will implement z using 0-based indices in code. Suppose we track a segment [l, r] (inclusive) where s[l..r] matches the prefix s[0..]. For i within [l, r], what is the best initial value for z[i] before trying to extend it?
mcqChain.diagram:
Current match window:
0 1 2 3 4 5 ...
s: p r e f i x ...
        ^ i in [l,r]
We know s[l..r] == s[0..r-l].
mcqChain.explanation: We can copy z[i-l] but cannot exceed the current window length r-i+1, so take the min.

wrongExplanations.1: Off by one: window length is r - i + 1.
wrongExplanations.2: z[i-l] may exceed the remaining window region.
wrongExplanations.3: Might be too large; it must be bounded by r - i + 1.

mcqChain.hint: You cannot claim matches beyond r without checking.

mcqChain.questionText: Using 0-based indexing, s = "aaaaa". After processing i=1, we have l=1, r=4 and z[1]=4. At i=2, what is the initial z[2] before extension?
mcqChain.diagram:
s: a a a a a
   0 1 2 3 4
After i=1: l=1, r=4.
i=2 is inside [1,4].

wrongExplanations.2: This ignores the min with the window length.
wrongExplanations.3: z[i-l] must be capped by r - i + 1.

mcqChain.hint: Use min(r - i + 1, z[i - l]).

mcqChain.questionText: Continuing s = "aaaaa" at i=2 (0-based). After the initial z[2]=3, can the while-loop extend further?

options.label: No, i + z[2] == n so cannot compare more.
It depends on the next character.

mcqChain.explanation: We must ensure i+z[i] < n to compare; here it's equal to n, so stop.

wrongExplanations.1: There is no next character to compare within bounds.

mcqChain.hint: Check the bounds condition of the extension loop.

mcqChain.questionText: For z in 0-based code: after computing z[i], when should we update the current [l, r] segment?

options.label: If i + z[i] - 1 > r, set l=i and r=i+z[i]-1
If i + z[i] >= r, set l=i and r=i+z[i]
If z[i] > 0, always set l=i and r=i+z[i]-1
Only when i > r, set l=i and r=i

mcqChain.explanation: We expand the known match window only if the new right boundary exceeds the previous r.

wrongExplanations.1: Off by one and allows equality which doesn’t extend the window.
wrongExplanations.2: Updating on every positive z[i] may shrink the window unnecessarily.

mcqChain.hint: Update only when the covered region grows.

mcqChain.questionText: For π in 0-based code, let j be the length of the current prefix match for s[0..i-1]. When s[i] mismatches s[j] and j>0, how should j be updated?

options.label: Set j = pi[j-1] and keep checking until match or j==0
Decrease j by 1 until it matches

mcqChain.explanation: We reuse the best smaller prefix length known for the previous position: j = pi[j-1], possibly repeatedly.

wrongExplanations.1: Sometimes a shorter border than 0 exists; don't drop all information immediately.
wrongExplanations.2: Subtracting 1 can miss larger valid shorter lengths.

mcqChain.hint: Use previously computed π values to jump to shorter candidates.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "ababaca" (0-based), what is π[5]? (Characters: a b a b a c a.)
mcqChain.explanation: At i=5 ('c'), mismatches at j=3, j becomes pi[2]=1, mismatch again so j becomes 0; thus π[5]=0.

wrongExplanations.3: The mismatch at 'c' causes fallbacks; no length-3 border remains.

mcqChain.hint: Trace j starting from π[4]=3 and fallback using π.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "aaaaa" (0-based), what is π[3]?
mcqChain.explanation: Each new 'a' extends the previous match by 1; π = [0,1,2,3,4], so π[3]=3.

wrongExplanations.1: It grows by one each step from the previous.

mcqChain.hint: When s[i] == s[j], set j = j + 1.

mcqChain.questionText: Read the input string efficiently. Choose the best snippet.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading one line and stripping removes the trailing newline. We need exactly the characters a–z.

wrongExplanations.1: Leaves the trailing newline, corrupting the string.
wrongExplanations.2: Reads the whole buffer including newline; extra characters remain.
wrongExplanations.3: May work but also leaves trailing newline if present; stripping is safer here.

mcqChain.hint: Ensure no newline remains in the string.

mcqChain.questionText: Implement the function to compute z for the entire string (0-based indexing in code). Pick the correct implementation.
mcqChain.explanation: It initializes from the current window, extends safely within bounds, and updates [l,r] only when the window grows.

wrongExplanations.1: Off-by-one in min and while bound; and window update is incorrect.
wrongExplanations.2: Misses the reuse step inside the window; becomes too slow in worst cases.
wrongExplanations.3: No reuse and no window update; quadratic behavior.

mcqChain.hint: Be careful with off-by-one in r - i + 1 and i + z[i] < n.

mcqChain.questionText: Implement the function to compute π for the entire string (0-based indexing in code). Choose the correct implementation.
mcqChain.explanation: It reuses previous π values to fallback (j = pi[j-1]) on mismatches and extends by 1 on a match.

wrongExplanations.3: This ignores repeated fallbacks; some strings need multiple jumps before matching.

mcqChain.hint: Fallback uses earlier π values, not j -= 1.

mcqChain.questionText: Call both functions to compute the arrays.
mcqChain.explanation: Compute z with the z function and π with the prefix function.

wrongExplanations.1: π must be computed with its own function.
wrongExplanations.3: z and π are different arrays and need their respective functions.

mcqChain.hint: Use each function for its corresponding array.

mcqChain.questionText: Print the z values for i=1..n on one line, space-separated.
mcqChain.explanation: The output must include z(1)=0 as defined; print all elements of z.

wrongExplanations.1: This skips z(1), but the output requires all positions 1..n.

mcqChain.hint: Do not drop the first element; z(1) is printed as 0.

mcqChain.questionText: Print the π values for i=1..n on the next line, space-separated.
mcqChain.explanation: We must output π for all positions including π(1)=0.
mcqChain.hint: Include all indices 1..n.

mcqChain.questionText: Run the solution on s = "abaabca". What should be printed?
mcqChain.explanation: Matches the example provided in the problem statement.

wrongExplanations.2: Check positions 6 and 7 carefully; the arrays differ there.

mcqChain.hint: Use the sample given to verify.

mcqChain.questionText: Run the solution on s = "aaaaa". What are the outputs?
mcqChain.explanation: z counts remaining matching prefix lengths; π increases by 1 at each step for identical characters.

wrongExplanations.1: π(1) must be 0; lengths are at most i−1.
wrongExplanations.2: z(1) is defined as 0; do not reorder.

mcqChain.hint: Think about repeated 'a' matching behavior for both arrays.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of computing both arrays with the implemented approach?

options.label: O(n) for z and O(n) for π, overall O(n)
O(n log n) for each, overall O(n log n)
O(n^2) for z and O(n) for π
O(n) for z and O(n^2) for π

mcqChain.explanation: Each index advances pointers without redoing work, so both scans are linear.

wrongExplanations.3: z also runs in linear time by reusing previous matches.

mcqChain.hint: Each character is compared only a constant number of times on average.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the extra space complexity of the solution (excluding the input string)?

options.label: O(n) for storing z and π

mcqChain.explanation: We allocate two arrays of length n; scalar variables are O(1).

wrongExplanations.1: We do allocate arrays proportional to n.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the arrays you store.

mcqChain.questionText: Edge case: n=1, s like "x". What should the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: By definition z(1)=0 and π(1)=0.

wrongExplanations.1: z(1) is always 0, and π(1) cannot exceed i−1=0.

mcqChain.hint: Both first values are defined as 0.

reasoningChain.question: What exactly do z(i) and π(i) measure on small examples?
reasoningChain.insight: Clarify definitions: z(i) compares s[i..] to the prefix; π(i) is the longest proper prefix ending at i.

reasoningChain.question: Why is checking from scratch at each position too slow?
reasoningChain.insight: Naive matching can be quadratic; we need to avoid redoing comparisons.

reasoningChain.question: How can we reuse previous matches for z to set a safe initial value?
reasoningChain.insight: Maintain a matched window [l,r]; if i is inside, start with min(r-i+1, z[i-l]).

reasoningChain.question: When and how do we extend and update the window for z?
reasoningChain.insight: Extend while characters match; if the new right boundary grows, set l=i and r=i+z[i]-1.

reasoningChain.question: How to update π efficiently on mismatch and match?
reasoningChain.insight: Use j as current matched length; on mismatch set j=pi[j-1] repeatedly; on match increment j.

reasoningChain.question: How to implement and print results efficiently for large n?
reasoningChain.insight: Single-pass functions with O(n) arrays and join-based printing for both lines.
